name: Sawyer, Ben D.
recent_1: Karwowski, W., Salvendy, G., Endsley, M., Rouse, W., Salmon, P., Stanney, K., Thatcher, A., Andre, T., Yang, J., Ayaz, H., Cakir, A., Duffy, V., Drury, C., Gao, Q., Guo, Y., Hancock, P., Marras, W. S., Rau, P., Sawyer, B., & Stanton, N. (2025). Grand challenges for human factors and ergonomics. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2024.2431336
recent_1_synopsis: A consensus position paper in which a large panel of senior human factors/ergonomics scholars (including Sawyer) articulates the grand challenges facing the discipline, spanning emerging technology, human-AI/autonomy interaction, systems safety, and societal impact. It matters as a field-defining agenda-setting statement that orients future HF/E research and Sawyer's inclusion signals recognition among the discipline's leading voices.
recent_2: Rashid, M. M., Atilgan, N., Dobres, J., Day, S., Penkova, V., Kucuk, M., Clapp, S. R., & Sawyer, B. D. (2024). Humanizing AI in education: A readability comparison of LLM and human-created educational content. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813241261689
recent_2_synopsis: This study compares the readability of educational content generated by large language models against human-authored content, applying readability metrics to assess how machine-generated text serves learners. It matters because it bridges Sawyer's readability/typography research program with the rapidly growing use of generative AI in education, informing how AI tools should be tuned for comprehension.
recent_3: Day, S. L., Atilgan, N., Giroux, A. E., & Sawyer, B. D. (2024). The influence of format readability on children's reading speed and comprehension. Education Sciences, 14(8), 854. DOI: 10.3390/educsci14080854
recent_3_synopsis: An empirical study examining how text formatting variables (such as font and character spacing) affect reading speed and comprehension in child readers. It matters because it extends the lab's individuated-readability findings from adults to children, supporting personalized digital-text interventions that could improve literacy outcomes for young readers.
seminal_1: Kaplan, A. D., Cruit, J., Endsley, M., Beers, S. M., Sawyer, B. D., & Hancock, P. A. (2021). The effects of virtual reality, augmented reality, and mixed reality as training enhancement methods: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 63(4), 706-726. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820904229
seminal_1_synopsis: A meta-analysis quantifying how VR, AR, and MR compare to traditional methods as training-enhancement tools across a large body of empirical studies. It is foundational and Sawyer's single most-cited work (~459 citations per Crossref, ~856 per Google Scholar as of June 2026), serving as a heavily referenced evidence base for the design and evaluation of immersive training systems.
seminal_2: Wallace, S., Bylinskii, Z., Dobres, J., Kerr, B., Berlow, S., Treitman, R., Kumawat, N., Arpin, K., Miller, D. B., Huang, J., & Sawyer, B. D. (2022). Towards individuated reading experiences: Different fonts increase reading speed for different individuals. ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction, 29(4), 1-56. DOI: 10.1145/3502222
seminal_2_synopsis: This large-scale study demonstrates that no single font is optimal for everyone and that matching font to the individual reader meaningfully increases reading speed without sacrificing comprehension. It is foundational to Sawyer's readability/personalization program (~54 citations per Crossref as of June 2026) and underpins the Readability Consortium's vision of reengineering digital typography for personalized reading.
expertise_overview: Sawyer is an applied neuroscientist and human factors engineer studying information exchange between humans and machines. His core research interests span driver attention and distraction, mental workload and cognitive failures, and human-AI/autonomy teaming, using methods including eye tracking, EEG/applied neuroscience, driving simulation, and mathematical/computational modeling of attention. A second major thread is readability and typography: through The Readability Consortium and Virtual Readability Lab he investigates how personalized fonts, character/word spacing, and text formatting affect reading speed and comprehension across adults and children. He also works on human factors of cybersecurity (e.g., the prevalence paradox) and, increasingly, the readability and human impact of generative AI content. His domains include transportation/automotive, education, cybersecurity, and immersive training (VR/AR/MR).
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. Confirmed via Google Scholar (user mynHwzkAAAAJ), UCF IEMS/CECS faculty pages, and personal site bendsawyer.com that this is Ben D. Sawyer, Associate Professor of Industrial Engineering at the University of Central Florida, director of LabX (Laboratory for Autonomy-brain Exchange) and The Readability Consortium / Virtual Readability Lab (formerly affiliated with MIT AgeLab). Research areas match the seed exactly (driver attention, human-AI interaction, readability/typography, workload, cybersecurity). All four pre-verified Human Factors anchor DOIs were re-confirmed against Crossref. Every DOI in this profile was verified to resolve (HTTP 200 + matching title) via api.crossref.org on 2026-06-17: recent_1 (10.1080/1463922X.2024.2431336), recent_2 (10.1177/10711813241261689), recent_3 (10.3390/educsci14080854), seminal_1 (10.1177/0018720820904229), seminal_2 (10.1145/3502222). Citation counts: Crossref is-referenced-by-count retrieved 2026-06-17 (meta-analysis 459; TOCHI 54); Google Scholar figures (meta-analysis ~856; total profile ~2,695) from web search snippet of the Scholar profile dated June 2026. Crossref and Scholar counts differ because Scholar captures broader/grey-literature citations; both are approximate. No invented DOIs; no unverifiable citations were included. Sources: Google Scholar, UCF faculty pages (iems.ucf.edu, cecs.ucf.edu), bendsawyer.com/publications, ResearchGate, Crossref API.
